coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon dots in combination with aqueous chitosan solution on shelf life and stability of soy milk
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Linlin
Zhang, Min
Wang, Haixiang
Devahastin, Sakamon
coredata.dc:description: Use of carbon dots (CDs) in combination with aqueous chitosan solution to extend shelf life and improve stability of soy milk was investigated. Soy milk samples with chitosan solution (0.00%, 0.08%, 0.12%, 0.16% and 0.20%) and banana-based CDs (4%, 6% and 8%) were prepared and stored at room temperature (25–30 °C) for shelf life evaluation. Soy milk with 0.16% chitosan solution exhibited improved stability as evident by increased viscosity, stability coefficient, zeta potential and decreased centrifugation rate compared with soy milk without chitosan. The suitable amount of carbon dots could effectively inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis. Soy milk with 0.16% chitosan and 8% CDs exhibited longer shelf life and significantly lower total bacterial count after storage at room temperature for up to 4 days. Electronic nose-based flavor characteristics of all treated soy milk samples were not far from that of the control sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus subtilis
Escherichia coli
Sensory evaluation
Shelf life
Stability
Staphylococcus aureus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability parameters of soy milk samples
Effect of chitosan solution on stability of soy milk samples
Synthesis, characterization and antibacterial effect of CDs
Color and flavor of soy milk samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology
Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R and D Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial supports from the China Key Research Programs (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501 ), the 111 Project ( BP0719028 ), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No. YZ2019034 ), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180205 ), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, all of which enabled us to carry out this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow diagram for the preparation of (a) soy milk and (b) CDs.
Changes in stability parameters of soy milk samples. (a) Viscosity, (b) centrifugation rate, (c) stability coefficient and (d) Zeta potential of chitosan samples.
Characterization of physical characteristics of CDs. (a) Particle size distribution, (b) TEM image, (c) FTIR spectrum, and (d) XRD pattern of CDs solution.

Antibacterial properties of CDs. (a) Histogram of bacterial growth versus CDs concentration after incubation for 24 h in liquid medium (b) Photographs of E. coli, S. aureus and B. subtilis colonies on solid medium (with different contents of CDs) after incubation for 24 h. The numbers below the photos represent bacterial colony counts.

Variation of different samples (control sample, sample with 0.16% CH, sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs) stored at room temperature for 0 days, 2 days and 4 days. Photo on the right side of each bottle is close-up view.
Sensor arrays and types in the electronic nose.
Mean sensory scores of soy milk samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Means ± SD with the same superscripts within a column are not significantly different (P > 0.05).
Note: L* indicates lightness, a* indicates redness/greenness, b* indicates yellowness/blueness. Means ± SD with the same superscripts within a column are not significantly different (P > 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: pH of soy milk samples during storage.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Means ± SD with the same superscripts within a column are not significantly different (P > 0.05). “–” indicates “not measured.”

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total bacterial count (TBC) of soy milk samples during storage.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon dots in combination with aqueous chitosan solution on shelf life and stability of soy milk

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Use of carbon dots (CDs) in combination with aqueous chitosan solution to extend shelf life and improve stability of soy milk was investigated. Soy milk samples with chitosan solution (0.00%, 0.08%, 0.12%, 0.16% and 0.20%) and banana-based CDs (4%, 6% and 8%) were prepared and stored at room temperature (25–30 °C) for shelf life evaluation. Soy milk with 0.16% chitosan solution exhibited improved stability as evident by increased viscosity, stability coefficient, zeta potential and decreased centrifugation rate compared with soy milk without chitosan. The suitable amount of carbon dots could effectively inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis. Soy milk with 0.16% chitosan and 8% CDs exhibited longer shelf life and significantly lower total bacterial count after storage at room temperature for up to 4 days. Electronic nose-based flavor characteristics of all treated soy milk samples were not far from that of the control sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Soybean is an important plant-based protein source as it contains all nine essential amino acids at the levels as high as those from egg, milk and meat (Nguyen et al., 2016). Soy-based foods have also received increasing attention after the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has confirmed the relation between soy protein and reduced risk of coronary heart disease (D'Adamo and Sahin, 2014).

Soy milk is a popular beverage prepared from soaked and ground soybean. Soy milk has a long history as a meaningful part of the East Asian cuisine and has recently achieved more acceptances in the Western countries. Since soy milk contains most of the soybean nutrients, including proteins, polysaccharides, lipids, minerals, vitamins and isoflavones (Subrota et al., 2013), it is acknowledged to provide a number of health benefits, including the ability to prevent the onset of certain types of cancers and the remission of hypertension (Rivas et al., 2002).

Despite its benefits, the shelf life of fresh soy milk is very short. This limits the consumption of fresh soy milk only to those consumers residing close to a production site. Processed soy milk is an obvious alternative to alleviate the above-mentioned limitation. Thermal treatment is the most typical process applied to enhance the microbiological safety and storage life of soy milk; such a treatment also helps enhance the nutritional value of soy milk by inactivating anti-nutritional factors and increasing protein digestibility (Riaz, 2005). Thermal treatment nevertheless adversely affects some quality characteristics (e.g., those related to heat-sensitive vitamins and bioactive compounds) of soy milk and causes distinct off-flavor (Amigo-Benavent et al., 2007). For this reason, it is always desirable to develop and/or investigate a milder process to improve the shelf life of soy milk. Using an appropriate antimicrobial agent is a possible means to inhibit proliferation of microorganisms, while maximizing the retention of soy nutrients during processing (Yuan et al., 2013).

Chitosan is a natural biopolymer obtained by deacetylation of chitin, which is the main constituent of crustacean shells and skeletons. Chitosan has widely been used in food processing due to its biocompatibility and non-toxicity (Borges et al., 2006). The antimicrobial activity of chitosan has also widely been demonstrated (Carrión-Granda et al., 2016; Mellegård et al., 2011; No et al., 2003). Zheng et al. (2017), for example, reported that chitosan at 5 g/L significantly inhibited gray (Botrytis cinerea) and blue mold (Penicillium expansum) in kiwifruit stored at 25 and 4 °C and exhibited beneficial effects on the fruit quality. A number of studies have also used chitosan in combination with other active substances to improve the shelf life of fresh food. Combination of chitosan and peony extracts were, for instance, noted to help inhibit the growth of native fungal microflora on strawberries, thus improving the quality and shelf life of the fruit (Pagliarulo et al., 2016). Combination of chitosan and ClO2 was noted to reduce the counts of total aerobic bacteria and yeasts and molds, thus extending the post-harvest life of fresh-cut bamboo shoot; the antimicrobial effect of the combined treatment was superior to that of ClO2 or chitosan treatment alone (Yang et al., 2015). The ability of chitosan as a thickener in food production has also been reported (Eduardo et al., 2014). Chitosan has nevertheless been used mostly as an antibacterial agent in films and coatings (Ma et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018); fewer studies focused on both antibacterial and stability enhancement properties of chitosan. In the present study, chitosan was added directly to soy milk rather than being used as a coating and both its antibacterial and thickening properties were studied.

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently received attention as a novel nanomaterial because of their non-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, cell membrane permeability, extraordinary opto-electrical properties, good photo-stability, and inexpensive nature with ease of availability (De and Karak, 2013; Fang et al., 2016). CDs have widely been used in photocatalysis, biological imaging, among other fields; increasing attention has also been paid to their antibacterial property. Some investigators have indeed modified CDs via doping and noted that the modified CDs exhibited high potential from microbial bacteriostatic and bactericidal points of view (Chen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016). Singh et al. (2017) recently proposed a newer approach to prepare curcumin quantum dots, which was noted to exhibit 90 times higher antimicrobial activity than the native curcumin. Again, most prior studies mainly focused on the bacteriostasis of CDs; very few studies exist on the use of CDs to prolong the shelf life of food. Application of CDs to prolong the shelf life of beverages has not been reported.

The purpose of the present investigation was to assess the application of chitosan as a thickening and antimicrobial agents in soy milk. Banana was selected as the starting material to synthesize CDs, which were then used in combination with aqueous chitosan solution as a natural bacteriostatic agent to extend the shelf life of soy milk. The preservation effect of soy milk was studied through various indices in order to explore a new technique for the preservation of soy milk.

Water-soluble chitosan (degree of deacetylation >90%; food grade) was purchased from Muhuang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Henan, China). Soybean, peanut, black soybean, sticky rice, black sesame, buckwheat, walnut, almond, cashew nuts and banana were purchased from a local supermarket in Wuxi, China.

The flow diagram illustrating the soy milk production process is shown in Fig. 1a. Soybean was first washed and soaked for 4 h at room temperature (25–30 °C). Hydrated soybean was ground using a blender (Yechun, ZKHB-WM-2200, Yangzhou, China). Then, auxiliary materials were added to soybean slurry. Soybean slurry produced was homogenized at 17 MPa, filtered through 100-mesh sieve to separate the water-soluble soy milk from the residue. The filtrate was boiled for 10 min to inactivate trypsin inhibitor, then hot-filled into pasteurized 250-mL glass bottles and screw capped.

Soy milk formulation was provided by Yechun Food Production and Distribution Co., Ltd.; the ingredients are as follows: 7.0% soybean, 3.2% peanut, 0.8% black soybean, 0.8% sticky rice, 0.5% black sesame, 0.2% buckwheat, 0.01% walnut, 0.01% almond, 0.01% cashew nuts, 3.5% sugar and distilled water. Based on this formulation, five groups of samples were prepared to study the effects of different concentrations of aqueous chitosan solution, i.e., 0%, 0.08%, 0.12%, 0.16%, 0.20% (w/v) on the stability of soy milk. Stability parameters of soy milk are stability coefficient, centrifugation rate, viscosity and zeta potential.

Sensory evaluation, which was used here only as a tentative evaluation, was performed in the Sensory Laboratory of the School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University. Ten untrained panels were selected for sensory evaluation. The evaluation was carried out under white fluorescent light at 25 °C. Forty milliliters of soy milk with different concentrations of chitosan solution in see-through plastic cups were provided to each panelist. Fresh cool drinking water was used to rinse the mouth between evaluations. Formulated soy milk samples were evaluated for their appearance (delamination and precipitation), mouth feel (viscosity attribute), flavor, color and overall acceptability. Each attribute was rated on a 9-point hedonic scale (from 1 = “Dislike extremely” to 9 = “Like extremely”) as suggested by Duan et al. (2017).

CDs were synthesized according to the methods of De and Karak (2013) with some modification. The preparation flow diagram is shown in Fig. 1b. Thirty grams of banana was first cut into small pieces and turned into paste with 90 mL of deionized water. Twenty milliliters of the paste and 20 mL of ethanol were placed in a 50-mL hydrothermal reaction kettle, which was then placed in an oven at 150 °C for 5 h. The product was then cooled at room temperature. The resulting aqueous solution was centrifuged at 3000 ×g for 20 min; supernatant was kept. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm), dialyzed in a 500-Da dialysis bag against deionized water to remove impurities and then freeze dried to obtain CDs powder.

Particle size of the CDs was measured after suitable dilution at 25 °C using a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). Morphology of the CDs was determined by a transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV. Sample was prepared for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by depositing one drop of CDs solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and then dried. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was determined by a FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray diffraction pattern was obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, D2 PHASER, Karlsruhe, Germany).

The antibacterial effect of CDs (CDs solution: 2 mg/mL) was tested in both liquid and solid media. Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) were cultivated in LB broth in a shaking incubator (37 °C, 180 rpm, 24 h). All bacterial suspensions were then treated with different contents of CDs (0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8% and 10%) and further cultivated (37 °C, 180 rpm, 24 h). After incubation, the optical density (OD) was measured by a spectrophotometer at 600 nm; the total bacterial count (TBC) was verified by the plate count method (solid agar without chitosan or CDs). Bacterial growth (%) was calculated by dividing TBC of different treatment groups by TBC of the control group. In addition, all bacterial suspensions were added to the surface of agar plates with different contents of CDs (0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8% and 10%). After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the formed bacterial colonies were photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability parameters of soy milk samples

ce:section.ce:para:
Stability coefficient was measured according to the methods of Mingming et al. (2018) with some modification. One milliliter of a soy milk sample was diluted to 1:40 using deionized distilled water. The absorbance of the mixture (2 mL) was then immediately determined at 785 nm (A 0). The residual mixture was centrifuged at 3000 ×g for 5 min; the absorbance was again determined at 785 nm (A). Stability coefficient (R) was calculated as:
R =
A
A 0
where R is the stability coefficient, R ≤ 1. The higher the R value, the more stable the soy milk system. A 0 is the absorbance of the sample prior to centrifugation, while A is the absorbance of the sample after centrifugation.

Centrifugation rate (CR) was measured according to the methods of Smith et al. (2009) with some modification. Fifteen milliliters of a soy milk sample was placed in a centrifugation tube (50 mL) and subject to centrifugation at 3000 ×g for 20 min. Supernatant was discarded, while the precipitate was dried in an oven to a constant mass. CR was calculated as follows:
CR =
M
M 0
× 100
where CR is the centrifugation rate, M is the mass of the precipitate (g), M 0 is the mass of 15-mL soy milk (g). The lower the CR value, the more stable the soy milk system.

Viscosity of a soy milk sample was immediately measured after the preparation by using a rotating-plate viscometer (Lichen Instruments, NDJ-8S, Shanghai, China) equipped with spindle No. 1 at 25 °C. A fixed volume of 100 mL for each sample was used and reading was taken at 60 rpm.

Zeta potential (ζ) of a soy milk sample was determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK) and calculated via the Smoluchowski model. Soy milk was subject to 10-fold dilution with deionized water prior to each measurement.

Five soy milk samples were prepared for shelf life evaluation. All samples were prepared according to the steps shown in Fig. 1a. After defibrination, CDs solution (2 mg/mL) and aqueous chitosan solution were added to soybean slurry at 60 °C to blend; this was followed by homogenization and other subsequent processes. Control sample (without chitosan and CDs solution), sample with 0.16% chitosan (0.16% CH (w/v)), samples with 0.16% chitosan and different levels of CDs solution (0.16% CH + 4% CDs, 0.16% CH + 6% CDs, 0.16% CH + 8% CDs (v/v)) were stored at room temperature (25–30 °C) and their stability was visually observed. Samples were inspected for quality attributes, including pH and TBC at a regular interval of 12 h.

Flavor of the treated soy milk was evaluated using an electronic nose (Ruifen Trading Co., iNose, Shanghai, China) immediately after preparation. Five milliliters of each sample was placed in a glass tube (50 mL). Fourteen metal oxide gas sensors as listed in Table 1 were used. The tested parameters were: 150 s of cleaning time, 150 s of testing time and 1 L/min of airflow. Color of the samples was observed via the use of a portable colorimeter (Konica-Minolta, CR-400, Osaka, Japan) immediately after the preparation. pH of the samples was measured using a pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, MP230, Greifensee, Switzerland).

TBC, which can offer information on the shelf life of a sample, was determined by diluting 10 mL of a soy milk sample with 90 mL of sterile normal saline. Ten-fold diluents were continuously diluted (1:10) in sterile normal saline. Three suitable dilutions were selected and 1 mL dilution was mixed with plate count agar (PCA). TBC was recorded after incubation at 37 ± 1 °C for 48 h and is expressed in CFU per mL (Moreira et al., 2011).

All determinations were performed in triplicate. Experimental data were subject to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and are presented as means ± standard deviations (SD). Significance of the means were established using Duncan's new multiple range tests at a confidence level of 95%. SPSS Version 19.0 was used for all statistical calculations. OriginPro 2016 was used to convert data into graphs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chitosan solution on stability of soy milk samples

ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2a, viscosity of soy milk samples increased as the chitosan concentration increased. The viscosity of the sample without chitosan was 14.65 mPa·s, while that of the sample with 0.20% chitosan was 39.57 mPa·s. Chitosan is a high polymer with large molecular weight; its molecular chains are relatively longer and can become entangled or form aggregates with each other to produce strong hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic effects. Therefore, it has a higher viscosity and a certain thickening effect. It has indeed been noted that such an increase in the viscosity can help reduce the probability of collisions between droplets in a system, so that the stability of a vegetable protein beverage can be improved (Mingming et al., 2018). It can also be seen in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c that the centrifugation rate decreased, while stability coefficient increased as the chitosan concentration increased. These results confirm that chitosan resulted in enhanced viscosity and stability of the freshly prepared samples, leading the products to have better acceptability in comparison with that without chitosan.

Surface charges on colloidal particles function as a major determinant of the magnitude and type of interactions among suspended particles (Song et al., 2013). Zeta potential (ζ) is one of the important parameters that can elucidate the magnitude of surface charges (Malhotra and Coupland, 2004). In our case, ζ can be used to indicate the strength of mutual repulsion or attraction between chitosan molecules. The greater the ζ is, the more charged the molecules are and the more stable the system is. Results shown in Fig. 2d indicated that the absolute ζ value of soy milk samples significantly increased (P < 0.05) with increasing chitosan concentration. The highest absolute ζ value was noted in the case of soy milk with 0.20% chitosan. Increased absolute ζ value with increased chitosan concentration is probably due to an increase in surface charges of particles, which in turn improved the repulsion among the suspended particles, resulting in disruption of protein aggregation.

Mean sensory scores of soy milk samples are shown in Table 2 . There were no significant differences (P > 0.05) among all samples in terms of flavor and color. In terms of appearance, the sample with 0.20% chitosan consistently rated higher than the other samples; score of the sample with 0.16% chitosan was lower than that of the sample with 0.20% chitosan but the difference between them was not significant (P > 0.05). In terms of mouth feel (viscosity attribute) and overall acceptability, the sample with 0.16% chitosan rated significantly higher than the others; the scores of the sample with 0.20% chitosan were extremely low. Testers indicated the taste of the sample with 0.20% chitosan as too sticky to be acceptable. In general, the sample with 0.16% chitosan exhibited better organoleptic quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization and antibacterial effect of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size distribution of CDs was first measured and is shown in Fig. 3a. The diameters of CDs from banana were mostly <1 nm. Similar results were noted by Fan et al. (2019) who indicated that sizes of CDs from kelp were 0.54–0.83 nm. Morphology of CDs as illustrated by a TEM image in Fig. 3b was noted to be monodispersed and spherical in nature.

FTIR spectrum for the identification of functional groups present in CDs is shown in Fig. 3c. CDs exhibited peaks at 3394 cm−1 (OH stretching vibrations), 2928 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CH bonds), 1701 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 (CO and CC stretching bending vibrations) and 1028 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO). XRD pattern of the CDs is shown in Fig. 3d. CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2Ɵ = 20.8°, which is attributed to the lattice spacing of graphitic carbon; this indicates that the CDs contained a graphite-like structure.

The antibacterial effect of CDs is shown in Fig. 4 . E. coli and S. aureus were selected as representative gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, respectively. Since some studies reported that B. subtilis is one of the bacteria that causes deterioration of soy milk (Adebayo-Tayo et al., 2009; Pascall et al., 2006), this bacterium was also selected for the study. In the liquid medium, growth of E. coli, S. aureus and B. subtilis was closely related to the content of CDs (Fig. 4a). With the CDs content increased from 0% to 10%, the bacterial growth decreased from 100% to 0%. The growth pattern of E. coli, S. aureus and B. subtilis in solid medium was similar to that in the liquid medium; the colony counts gradually decreased with the increasing CDs content (Fig. 4b). Only a small number of bacteria were able to reproduce on the solid plates containing 8% CDs (8, 11, and 61 CFU/mL for E. coli, S. aureus and B. subtilis). When the content of CDs reached 10%, there were no colonies of any kinds of bacteria on the plates.

Note that while it is true that carbon dots have an inhibitory effect on yeasts and molds as has been reported in one of our previous publications (Fan et al., 2019), we only focused our current attention on verifying the broad-spectrum antibacterial effect of carbon dots. The control group without added carbon dots could be regarded as a positive control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color and flavor of soy milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is seen in Table 3 that, compared with the control sample, L* and b* values of the sample with 0.16% chitosan decreased by 2.54 and 0.12, while a* value increased by 0.06. With increasing concentration of CDs, the values of L *, a* and b* of the samples were on a downward trend. Color differences among the different samples were not distinguishable by eyes, so these changes should be acceptable.

It is seen from the electronic nose data (Fig. 5a) that, except for the sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs, all sensors reported no obvious differences among the different samples. The sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs resulted in the lower S4, S6, S7, S9, S10, S12 and S13 signals compared with the control sample, indicating slight flavor change.

Principal component analysis (PCA) was used to discriminate the differences among various samples (Fig. 5b). The principal component 1 and principal component 2 were 89.0% and 6.0% of the total sensor response variance, respectively. The cumulative contribution rate of the two components was 95.0%, indicating that the differences of all soy milk samples could be distinguished. Quantitatively, however, the flavor characteristics of all soy milk samples were not far from that of the control sample.

As listed in Table 4 , at room temperature (25–30 °C), pH of the control sample decreased from 6.70 to 5.64 after 36 h, pH of the samples with 0.16% CH and with 0.16% CH + 4% CDs decreased to 5.34 and 5.77 after 72 h, pH of the sample with 0.16% CH + 6% CDs decreased to 5.82 after 84 h, while pH of the sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs remained above 6.5 during the storage period of 96 h.

Changes in soy milk pH are reported to be caused by microbial activity leading to lipolysis, proteolysis and fermentation of sugars (Navicha et al., 2018). As the concentration of CDs increased, pH of soy milk samples decreased. This is due to the lower pH value of the CDs solution. Lower pH value is nevertheless not conducive to the stability of soy milk, so it is necessary to adjust the pH of a sample during the real production.

According to the hygienic standard for vegetable protein beverage (China National Standard GB 16322-2003), TBC of soy milk product shall not exceed 100 CFU/mL. As shown in Table 5 , in the control sample, TBC exceeded 100 CFU/mL after 24 h of storage at room temperature, indicating that the shelf life of this soy milk was <1 day. Similar results were noted by Khodke et al. (2014) who reported that fresh soy milk samples were acceptable only up to 16 h post preparation at ambient temperature.

TBC of the sample with 0.16% chitosan did not exceed 100 CFU/mL after 48 h of storage. These results correspond to the well-known fact that chitosan has a certain antibacterial effect and can prolong the storage life of soy milk to 2 days. Zivanovic et al. (2004) who studied the antibacterial effect of chitosan at different concentrations also indicated that adding 0.1% chitosan was adequate to guarantee microbiological safety of their oil-in-water emulsion. The antibacterial efficacy of chitosan is owing to the interaction between its active amino group and negatively charged cell membrane of microorganisms; this leads to cell membrane damage and losses of cytoplasm from the microbial cells (Dutta et al., 2009). Besides, chitosan has been confirmed to be capable of disrupting the lipo-polysaccharide layer of gram-negative bacteria (Gerasimenko et al., 2004).

TBC of the sample with 0.16% CH + 6% CDs did not exceed 100 CFU/mL after 72 h of storage. There was no bacterial growth in 0.16% CH + 8% CDs sample up to 84 h. Thus, CDs in combination with chitosan could effectively extend the shelf life of our soy milk, but on a dosage-dependence fashion. Soy milk sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs exhibited the longest shelf life at room temperature. It is speculated that the antibacterial activity of CDs is partly attributed to the damage to the membrane structure of the bacteria. Singh et al. (2017) found that CDs prepared from curcumin (CurQDs) could degrade biofilms and inhibit biogenesis of the biofilms. CurQDs had more obvious effect on biofilms with higher protein contents, and its inhibitory effect on biofilms was dose-dependent. Jian et al. (2017) also supported the above speculation that the antibacterial effect of CDs is related to the destruction of cell membrane. Travlou et al. (2018) later reported that the antimicrobial activity of CDs is related to their surface chemical properties and size in the nanometer range. High TBC contents (>100 and 20 CFU/mL) obtained in samples with CH + CDs, while they were not detected before. This may be due to the low concentration of bacteria in soy milk in the early stage, and the samples did not contain bacteria (or very few bacteria). In the later stage, with the growth of bacteria, the samples contained more bacteria, and then it can be detected.

Alteration in the appearance of soy milk samples stored at room temperature was observed. Control sample, sample with 0.16% CH and sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs were compared. As is seen in Fig. 6 , after 2 days of storage, precipitation was observed in the control sample, while the samples with 0.16% CH and 0.16% CH + 8% CDs showed better storage stability. After 4 days of storage, color of the control sample and sample with 0.16% CH changed; no color change and no precipitation were observed in the sample with 0.16% CH + 8% CDs. This indicates that CDs in combination with aqueous chitosan solution could effectively extend the shelf life and improve the stability of soy milk.

Under the experimental conditions used in this study, the shelf life of soy milk could be prolonged for at least 4 days at room temperature. Good microbial stability was noted for soy milk during the storage period, indicating that CDs in combination with aqueous chitosan solution as bacteriostatic agent is effective. Such a combination was capable of extending the shelf life and positively affected the stability of soy milk. Combined use of CDs and chitosan therefore has potential to be an alternative means to conventional heat treatment for improving the shelf life of soy milk. Appropriate adjustment of pH of a sample during the production may need attention, however.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial supports from the China Key Research Programs (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501), the 111 Project (BP0719028), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No. YZ2019034), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180205), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, all of which enabled us to carry out this study.
